title: AcyclicUnderIntervention

lean_statement.name: isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_3 part A: if `G` is acyclic then so is
`G.hardInterventionOn W`, for *any* `W : Set α`. Mirrors the first
half of the `\Rem` immediately after def_3_10 in
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (line 311).

We split the LN's single `\Rem` into two theorems because the
acyclicity and topological-order halves carry different
hypotheses, and different downstream consumers pattern-match on
different halves. `claim_3_4` ("hard interventions commute") and
the iSCM uniqueness theory of chapters 8 -- 10 need acyclicity of
the intervened graph *alone* -- they have no named topological
order on hand, so a bundled conjunction would force them either
to project, or to drag in the `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition that
part B (`isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn`) requires but
part A does not. The downstream consumers of part B in
`do_calculus.tex` / `id_algorithm.tex` carry a named `<` through
long proofs and likewise prefer the relation-level form on its
own, without the acyclicity conjunct.

No `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition appears here because every
directed edge of `G.hardInterventionOn W` is a directed edge of
`G` (by `mem_hardInterventionOn_E` in `HardInterventionOn.lean`),
so directed walks in the intervened graph lift to walks of the
same length in `G` and any cycle would lift to a cycle in `G`.
Spurious vertices in `W \ (G.J ∪ G.V)` participate in no edges
at all (by `G.E_subset` on the source / `G.L_subset` on both
endpoints), so they cannot host a cycle either. For the same
reason no `[Finite α]` instance is required -- the walk-lifting
argument is purely structural (edges shrink under intervention),
and adding finiteness would block downstream use in iSCM
chapters working over not-yet-finitised vertex types. `W` is an
*explicit* binder because consumers (claim_3_4, claim_3_5,
iSCMs) typically have a specific `W` in mind that is not
unifiable from elsewhere, while `G` is implicit because it is
recovered from `(G.hardInterventionOn W).IsAcyclic`. The name
follows Mathlib's `<conclusion>_<construction>` convention --
the `IsAcyclic` predicate first, then the `hardInterventionOn`
construction we are showing preserves it. -/
theorem isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG α} (W : Set α) (h : G.IsAcyclic) :
    (G.hardInterventionOn W).IsAcyclic := by

lean_statement.name: isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_3 part B: if `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` and `r` is a topological
order of `G`, then `r` is also a topological order of
`G.hardInterventionOn W`. Mirrors the second half of the `\Rem`
immediately after def_3_10 in
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (line 311); see
`isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn` above for the rationale behind
splitting that `\Rem` into two theorems and for the downstream
consumers of the acyclicity half.

The `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition that was *absent* in part A is
*essential* here: `IsTopologicalOrder G' r` quantifies its four
fields (`irrefl` / `trans` / `trichotomous` / `parent_lt`) over
the nodes of `G'`, and `(G.hardInterventionOn W).J ∪
(G.hardInterventionOn W).V = (G.J ∪ W) ∪ (G.V \ W)` collapses to
`G.J ∪ G.V` only when `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`. Without the precondition
a vertex `v ∈ W \ (G.J ∪ G.V)` would be a node of the intervened
graph that `hr.irrefl` / `hr.trichotomous` say nothing about,
breaking those fields in general. Part A escaped this issue
because its walk-lifting argument only touches
`(G.hardInterventionOn W).E ⊆ G.E`, never the node set of the
intervened graph.

`W` and `r` are both implicit because they are unifiable from the
hypotheses (`hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` and
`hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r`) and from the conclusion
`.IsTopologicalOrder r`; downstream callers in
`do_calculus.tex` / `id_algorithm.tex` carry a fixed named `<`
through long proofs and pass it through `hr`, so they never need
to spell `r` (or `W`) at the call site -- they simply write
`isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn hW hr`. `G` is implicit
for the same reason it is in part A. Naming follows Mathlib's
`<conclusion>_<construction>` convention, consistent with part A
and with the `IsTopologicalOrder` predicate's own naming in
`Section3_1/TopologicalOrder.lean`. -/
theorem isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG α} {W : Set α} {r : α → α → Prop}
    (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) (hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r) :
    (G.hardInterventionOn W).IsTopologicalOrder r := by

lean_explanation:
Both theorems fix a CDMG `G : CDMG α` over an ambient vertex type `α : Type*` — the LN's silent "let $G$ be a CDMG" carries no built-in finiteness, and none is needed here. `W : Set α` is the set of vertices being intervened on, matching the LN's $W$ in $G_{\doit(W)}$; in Lean the intervened graph is written `G.hardInterventionOn W`. Part A additionally takes `(h : G.IsAcyclic)`, with `IsAcyclic` (def_3_6) expressing "no non-trivial directed walk closes into a cycle". Part B additionally takes a candidate relation `r : α → α → Prop`, the precondition `(hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V)`, and the hypothesis `(hr : G.IsTopologicalOrder r)` — `IsTopologicalOrder` (def_3_8) is the four-field bundle of `irrefl` / `trans` / `trichotomous` on $J \cup V$, plus `parent_lt` saying every parent precedes its child.

Two Mathlib idioms are worth flagging. First, `Set.Subset` (`W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`) and the set-builder operations `G.J ∪ G.V`, `G.V \ W` underneath `hardInterventionOn` are pure Mathlib set-theory; def_3_10 defines the intervened graph by `J_{\doit(W)} = J \cup W` and `V_{\doit(W)} = V \setminus W`, so the LN-level membership `v \in G_{\doit(W)}` unfolds to the union $(J \cup W) \cup (V \setminus W)$. Second, `IsTopologicalOrder` is a relation-level predicate (the property of a specific `r`), not the existential `HasTopologicalOrder`; the LN's "a topological order for $G$ is also one for $G_{\doit(W)}$" most naturally reads as the relation-level transport — the *same* `r` works for both graphs — and that is what part B states.

The shape is two independent transport theorems, each taking a property of `G` and concluding the same property of `G.hardInterventionOn W`. Part A requires no precondition on `W`; part B requires `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`. In both, `G` is implicit because it is recoverable from the conclusion, while the binder choices for `W` and `r` differ between the two parts for ergonomic reasons explained in `design_choices`.
design_choices:
**The LN's single `\Rem` is split into two theorems with different hypotheses.** Forré–Mooij bundle the two conclusions ("$G_{\doit(W)}$ is acyclic" and "a topological order for $G$ is also one for $G_{\doit(W)}$") under one paragraph. Lean splits them because they carry different preconditions and because their downstream consumers want different halves. `claim_3_4` ("hard interventions commute") and the iSCM uniqueness theory of chapters 8 – 10 need acyclicity of the intervened graph *without* any named topological order on hand; the do-calculus and ID-algorithm proofs of chapters 5 – 6 carry a fixed named `<` and want the relation-level form *without* an acyclicity conjunct. A single bundled conjunction would force every caller either to project or to drag in the `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition the acyclicity half does not need.

**Part A takes no `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` precondition; part B does.** The asymmetry is structural. The acyclicity argument only ever uses the edge inclusion `(G.hardInterventionOn W).E ⊆ G.E` (by `mem_hardInterventionOn_E`): any directed walk in the intervened graph lifts to a directed walk of the same length in `G`, so a cycle there would be a cycle in `G`. Spurious vertices in `W \ (G.J ∪ G.V)` get promoted to inputs but participate in no edges at all (by `G.E_subset` / `G.L_subset`), so they cannot host a cycle either. The topological-order argument, by contrast, transports the four `IsTopologicalOrder` fields by quantifier instantiation over the *node set* of the intervened graph `(G.J ∪ W) ∪ (G.V \ W)`, which only collapses to `G.J ∪ G.V` under `W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`. Without that precondition a vertex in `W \ (G.J ∪ G.V)` would sit in the intervened graph's node set but be unconstrained by `hr`, breaking `irrefl` / `trichotomous` in general.

**No `[Finite α]` typeclass anywhere.** Acyclicity-preservation in part A is a structural walk-lifting argument that does not enumerate vertices; the topological-order transport in part B is a quantifier-level manipulation of the four fields of `IsTopologicalOrder`. Neither needs finiteness, and adding it would block downstream use in iSCM chapters where vertex types are real-valued and not yet known to be finite at the point of application.

**`W` is explicit in part A, implicit in part B.** Part A's typical callers (`claim_3_4`, `claim_3_5`, iSCM chapters) hold a specific `W` in mind that is not unifiable from anywhere else in the call, so making `W` explicit lets them write `isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn W h` directly. Part B's `W` is recoverable by unification from the precondition `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`, so it is left implicit — callers in `do_calculus.tex` / `id_algorithm.tex` carrying a named `<` through long proofs simply write `isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn hW hr` without ever spelling `W` or `r`.

**Naming follows Mathlib's `<conclusion>_<construction>` convention.** `isAcyclic_hardInterventionOn` reads "the `IsAcyclic` predicate, applied to the `hardInterventionOn` construction"; `isTopologicalOrder_hardInterventionOn` does the same for `IsTopologicalOrder`. The convention scales: the next rows on this construction (`HardInterventionsCommute`, `HardInterventionNodeSplittingCommute`, etc.) reuse the same suffix to signal "a fact about `hardInterventionOn`".
